aliases: Samosa with Chickpea Curry and Chutneys

english: Samosa with Chickpea Curry and Chutneys

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for samosa as a food product; samosa chaat is a composite dish with additional toppings (chole, yogurt, chutneys) not captured by the base samosa entry. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated samosa chaat code. A0FKP (samosa) used as the closest defensible anchor. The chaat assembly with chole, yogurt, and chutneys is not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for samosa chaat as a composite dish.

main_ingredients: samosa (wheat flour pastry shell, potato filling, spices)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of samosa chaat is one plate containing one to two samosas broken open and topped with chole curry, yogurt, tamarind chutney, green chutney, and sev, weighing approximately 200 g.

nutrient_source.title: Samosa Chat — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00011068 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for samosa (NDB 18390) as a secondary reference; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for samosa chaat as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low; GI ≤55 is classified as low per the University of Sydney GI Database classification system) based on the dominant carbohydrate components: wheat flour pastry (GI ~55, analogous to white wheat bread/pastry), boiled potato filling (GI ~56–78 depending on preparation), and chickpea chole (GI ~28–36 for boiled chickpeas). The composite GI is weighted toward the pastry and potato components which dominate the carbohydrate load (33.16 g/100g), with the chickpea component providing a meaningful downward pull. A blended estimate of GI 55 is used, which sits at the upper boundary of the low GI category (≤55) per University of Sydney classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 66.32 g per 200 g serving) / 100 = 36.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 388 mg/100 g with low potassium offset (166 mg/100 g) and low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g); chaat masala and tamarind chutney toppings further elevate sodium beyond the base measurement.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit samosa chaat to a small occasional portion, request reduced chaat masala and chutneys, avoid sev topping, and account for the cumulative sodium contribution within their daily 1500–2000 mg sodium target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (33.16 g/100 g) from wheat flour pastry and potato filling with low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g), yielding a high glycaemic load of approximately 36 per standard serving; sweet tamarind chutney adds concentrated sugars.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit samosa chaat due to its high glycaemic load; if consumed, request omission of tamarind chutney and sev, limit to half a portion, and pair with a high-fibre side to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 17.47 g/100 g including saturated fat approximately 3.8 g/100 g and trans fat 0.2 g/100 g from deep-frying; sodium 388 mg/100 g and cholesterol 14 mg/100 g further elevate cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat samosa chaat as an occasional food only, given its combination of deep-fried saturated and trans fats, high sodium, and high glycaemic load, all of which are independently associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); potato and green chilli are nightshade vegetables contributing solanine; trans fat 0.2 g/100 g is pro-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid samosa chaat due to its nightshade vegetable content, AGE-generating deep-frying method, and trans fat content, all of which may exacerbate joint inflammation; request a baked preparation if available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Samosa chaat (also spelled samosa chat) is a popular Indian street food and chaat dish widely available in Singapore's Indian food stalls and hawker centres. It is assembled by breaking open one or two deep-fried samosas — triangular wheat flour pastry shells filled with spiced mashed potato and sometimes peas — and topping them generously with spiced chickpea curry (chole), plain yogurt, sweet tamarind chutney, green coriander-mint chutney, chaat masala spice blend, and crispy sev (thin fried chickpea noodles). The result is a complex layered dish combining hot, cold, sweet, sour, spicy, and savoury flavours simultaneously. The texture contrasts the crispy pastry shell with the soft potato filling, creamy yogurt, and crunchy sev. Nutritionally, samosa chaat is energy-dense at 310 kcal/100 g, with a high carbohydrate load of 33.16 g/100 g from refined wheat pastry and potato, moderate fat at 17.47 g/100 g from deep-frying, and elevated sodium at 388 mg/100 g. Dietary fibre is low at 1.8 g/100 g. The glycaemic load per standard 200 g serving is approximately 36, making it a high-glycaemic-load food despite a GI of 55 at the low-medium boundary. It is not suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without significant portion restriction and topping modification. It is vegetarian and halal-friendly but contains gluten and dairy.

confidence_reason: Core per_100g nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id G00011068, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy samosa analogue. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database components. All other values preserved from the canonical HPB record.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.8 g/100 g) is not reported in the HPB source for this entry; it is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for samosa (NDB 18390) as the closest approved-source analogue. This value should be replaced with a direct HPB or ICMR-NIN measurement when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for samosa chaat is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this composite dish as of 2026. 3. trans_fat_g (0.2 g/100 g) is carried forward from the input health_suitability clinical notes; this value is not explicitly reported in the HPB per_100g table and should be verified against a direct source. 4. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB source. 5. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: micronutrient panel beyond the HPB-reported fields is not available in the HPB Foodo database for this composite dish as of 2026. 6. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for samosa chaat exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI ≤55 is classified as low; GI 55 sits at the upper boundary of the low category. gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. 7. Chinese name 萨摩萨聊天 / 薩摩薩聊天 is a transliteration approximation; no established Chinese culinary name exists for this dish in Singapore. A food specialist should verify or replace with a community-accepted Chinese rendering.

Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' for FoodOn and FoodEx2.

Per University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI ≤55 is classified as low; GI 55 sits at the upper boundary of the low category. Updated gi_source notes and record notes to document this classification rationale.
